narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 48.2% chance that you would not pass. This probability crossed our threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with the strongest projected odds of bar success to maintain high pass rates. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your class—far below the typical fifth-decile average among students who went on to pass the bar. This low ranking was the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model a potential struggle with sustaining strong performance over time. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 35, which, while not the lowest possible, fell short of the 37.27 average for bar passers and pulled somewhat against failure by showing solid reasoning ability. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 also contributed positively, nudging the prediction toward passing since it exceeded what the model sometimes sees in struggling applicants, though it trailed the 3.24 average of successful bar takers. Coming from a high family income background, which is less common among bar passers (only about 8.5% share this), and planning full-time enrollment didn't sway things much, as those align more closely with typical admitted profiles. Overall, your profile stacks up weaker than most accepted students, especially in later-year grades, where bar success patterns are clearest. The good news is this prediction isn't destiny—many students improve dramatically with targeted effort. To strengthen a future application, focus on boosting your academic decile through upper-level coursework, perhaps by seeking tutoring or study groups tailored to law school rigor. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score could also tip the scales, given its strong role here. Consider gaining practical experience, like internships, to build a more competitive narrative, and reapply once you've addressed these gaps—we'd be glad to see your growth.
